svg.title: Global Context in E-Waste

rect.data-narration: The rapid growth of electronic devices is driven by decreasing production costs and increasing global demand.

path.data-narration: Globalization and e-commerce have created new markets for electronic waste, leading to increased e-waste generation.

rect.data-narration: Lack of effective e-waste management infrastructure and limited public awareness are significant challenges in e-waste management.

path.data-narration: Unauthorized dumping and burning of e-waste are common practices in many countries.

rect.data-narration: E-waste contains toxic substances that can contaminate soil, water, and air, posing significant health risks to humans.

path.data-narration: Burning e-waste releases toxic fumes and particulate matter, contributing to air pollution and climate change.

rect.data-narration: E-waste can have significant economic costs, including cleanup and disposal costs, as well as economic impacts of toxic substances released into the environment.

path.data-narration: E-waste can also have significant economic costs, including cleanup and disposal costs, as well as economic impacts of toxic substances released into the environment.

rect.data-narration: The Basel Convention and the Electronic Waste (E-Waste) Guidelines are international efforts to reduce the export of hazardous waste, including e-waste.

path.data-narration: The Basel Convention aims to reduce the export of hazardous waste, including e-waste, while the Electronic Waste (E-Waste) Guidelines promote sustainable e-waste management practices.

svg.footer: Learn more about the global context of e-waste and how you can contribute to reducing electronic waste.
